title: Implement upgrade asset state evaluation helpers
description: Add CLI-side helpers that compute the bundled asset catalog, compare manifest state against current disk contents, detect user-added assets, and classify per-file upgrade actions including update, new, restored, skipped, and disabled outcomes.
acceptance_criteria: Helpers can determine whether a tracked file is unmodified, locally modified, missing, user-owned, or removed from the current bundle.
Bundled assets removed from the new release are mapped to a `.disabled` action instead of deletion.
User-added files outside the bundled catalog are identified for manifest tracking as `source: user` and `user_owned: true`.
Dry-run mode can reuse the same decision logic without performing writes.

handoff_summary.completed: Added to onboarding.py: AssetDecision dataclass with all action fields, _compute_bundled_catalog() enumerating all 4 bundled asset roots (templates/agents/, .agents/skills/, .claude/skills/, .takt/config.yaml), evaluate_upgrade_actions() implementing the full 8-action decision table. Replaced command_upgrade() and command_asset() stubs in cli.py with full implementations. command_upgrade() uses dry_run flag to control writes while sharing the same decision logic. command_asset() implements mark-owned, unmark-owned, and list subcommands. All disabled assets are renamed to .disabled (not deleted). User-added files are tracked as source:user, user_owned:true.
handoff_summary.remaining: Config key-merge step (merge_config_keys for .takt/config.yaml deep-merge) is not implemented — that is scope for a downstream bead per the spec. The command_upgrade stub references it in its docstring but the spec separates it. takt init manifest integration (writing manifest at end of scaffold_project) is handled by bead B-e69881cf.
handoff_summary.risks: command_upgrade writes the updated manifest with an added upgraded_at field — downstream tester should verify this field does not break read_assets_manifest which expects only takt_version, installed_at, assets keys. The _compute_bundled_catalog function uses packaged_*_dir() helpers which rely on importlib.resources — verify behavior in wheel-installed context vs editable install.
handoff_summary.next_action: Run tester bead to verify all action types, dry-run behavior, and user-added file detection.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: onboarding.py is also claimed by bead B-e69881cf (manifest schema helpers). That bead is still in ready status and may add write_assets_manifest/read_assets_manifest — both are already present in the current worktree, so merging should be clean. cli.py is also claimed by B-9e0bf6cf (CLI parser scaffolding) which is also still ready — the scaffold (parser + stub functions) is already in place, so no conflict expected.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: Evaluation logic placed in onboarding.py (alongside manifest helpers) rather than cli.py so it is importable from tests without pulling in the full CLI. command_upgrade imports the helpers lazily (inside the function body) following the pattern already used by command_init. Added "unchanged" action (disk == manifest sha == bundled sha) to avoid printing [updated] when nothing actually changed in the bundle — improves dry-run output clarity without contradicting the spec's decision table. _compute_bundled_catalog() returns {rel_path: abs_path} (not just paths) so command_upgrade can call shutil.copy2 directly from the catalog without re-resolving paths. disabled action removes the manifest entry rather than tracking the .disabled filename — the .disabled file is user territory and should not be managed by subsequent upgrade runs.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: Test each of the 8 AssetDecision action types: update, unchanged, new, restored, skipped_user_owned, skipped_modified, disabled, user_added. Test dry_run=True produces no writes but same printed output. Test command_asset mark-owned with fnmatch glob matching multiple paths. Test command_asset list columns and coloring. Test evaluate_upgrade_actions with empty manifest (all entries are new). Test that user-added files under .agents/skills/ not in bundle or manifest get action=user_added with user_owned=True. Test that disabled action renames file to .disabled and removes manifest key. Test that the upgraded_at field is written to manifest on successful upgrade.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: Config key-merge (merge_config_keys for .takt/config.yaml deep-merge) is intentionally not implemented here per spec decomposition — it is a separate concern. command_upgrade currently applies the standard hash-based logic to .takt/config.yaml (skipped_modified if user has edited it, which they almost certainly have). The config merge bead should override this behavior. The "unchanged" action is silently skipped in non-dry-run mode (no output line) — this is intentional to avoid noisy output for large unmodified installs, but could be a source of confusion if users expect acknowledgment for every file.

conflict_risks: onboarding.py is also claimed by bead B-e69881cf (manifest schema helpers). That bead is still in ready status and may add write_assets_manifest/read_assets_manifest — both are already present in the current worktree, so merging should be clean. cli.py is also claimed by B-9e0bf6cf (CLI parser scaffolding) which is also still ready — the scaffold (parser + stub functions) is already in place, so no conflict expected.

execution_history.summary: Implemented upgrade asset state evaluation helpers. Added `AssetDecision` dataclass, `_compute_bundled_catalog()`, and `evaluate_upgrade_actions()` to `onboarding.py`. Implemented full `command_upgrade()` and `command_asset()` in `cli.py` using these helpers. The evaluation logic is pure (no side effects) so dry-run reuses the same decision path without writes.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `{{LANGUAGE}}` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: `{{BUILD_CHECK_COMMAND}}`.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `{{TEST_COMMAND}}` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
